name: ARM MTE — Memory Tagging Extension
summary: ARM's hardware memory-tagging mechanism: 4-bit tag per 16-byte granule stored both in pointer top bits (TBI) and in shadow tag memory; hardware checks on every load/store. SYNC / ASYNC / ASYMM modes trade precision for speed. Shipping on Pixel 8/9 and other flagship Android SoCs as of 2026-04.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
ARM's **Memory Tagging Extension** stores a 4-bit tag per 16-byte granule, both in pointer top bits (Top-Byte-Ignore / TBI) and in shadow tag memory; hardware checks on every load/store. The mechanism is the production-silicon descendant of the SPARC ADI design point and the architectural counterpart of HWASan's software tagging.

MTE sits at the *per-allocation tagging* point in the hardware-assisted memory-safety design space — orthogonal to PAC's pointer integrity, CET's control-flow integrity, MPK's per-page protection groups, and CHERI's full bounded-capability model.

sections.title: SYNC / ASYNC / ASYMM modes
sections.content:
SYNC / ASYNC / ASYMM modes trade precision for speed:

- **SYNC** traps immediately on tag mismatch with the faulting PC and exact address.
- **ASYNC** accumulates faults to a status register checked on context switch.
- **ASYMM** combines synchronous-on-load with asynchronous-on-store.

Scudo allocator integration randomises tags on malloc/free for use-after-free and linear-overflow detection. Stack tagging is provided by the `AArch64StackTagging` compiler instrumentation pass.

sections.title: Overhead, deployment, and TIKTAG
sections.content:
Overhead: Scudo + MTE SYNC ≈ 12% geomean SPECrate, MTE ASYNC ≈ 4% (NanoTag, 2025). Originally marketed as "near-zero" — real cost is non-trivial in SYNC.

Status (as of 2026-04): shipping on Pixel 8/9 (opt-in) and upcoming flagship Android SoCs; Apple MIE on recent A-series devices is covered separately as a more aggressive consumer deployment combining EMTE with secure typed allocators.

The **TIKTAG attack** (2024) demonstrated speculative tag leakage on Pixel 8 — the same class of vulnerability that Apple's Tag Confidentiality Enforcement was designed to mitigate.
